intent: Define the SecurityRecipes MCP authorization conformance model so agent hosts, MCP gateways, IAM teams, and enterprise buyers can verify resource-bound tokens, token audience validation, PKCE, protected-resource metadata, token-passthrough denial, scope minimization, consent, session binding, and audit evidence before agents use MCP tools.

positioning.buyer: AI Platform, IAM, Product Security, GRC, procurement security, and enterprise architecture teams
positioning.category: MCP authorization conformance and scope-drift control
positioning.enterprise_promise: SecurityRecipes can explain whether MCP tool access is bound to the right resource, token audience, client, workflow, agent run, and scope before a tool call reaches enterprise systems.
positioning.why_now: MCP adoption is moving from local developer experiments to remote production servers. The enterprise control gap is now authorization evidence: who delegated which agent, to which MCP resource, with which scopes, and whether raw user tokens or over-broad permissions leaked into the tool path.

standards_alignment.name: Model Context Protocol Authorization
standards_alignment.coverage: OAuth authorization for HTTP MCP servers, resource indicators, protected-resource metadata, authorization-server metadata, PKCE, HTTPS, bearer-token validation, and token audience checks.

standards_alignment.name: Model Context Protocol Security Best Practices
standards_alignment.coverage: Confused-deputy prevention, token-passthrough avoidance, SSRF controls, scope minimization, session safety, local-server hardening, and audit trails.

standards_alignment.name: OWASP Top 10 for Agentic Applications 2026
standards_alignment.coverage: Agent goal hijacking, tool misuse, identity and privilege abuse, agentic supply-chain risk, unexpected code execution, memory and context poisoning, and rogue-agent containment.

standards_alignment.name: OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications 2025
standards_alignment.coverage: Prompt injection, sensitive information disclosure, supply-chain risk, improper output handling, excessive agency, vector weakness, and unbounded consumption.

standards_alignment.name: CISA AI Data Security Best Practices
standards_alignment.coverage: Data provenance, integrity, access control, monitoring, third-party data handling, evidence, and incident-response expectations for AI systems.

standards_alignment.name: CISA Secure by Design
standards_alignment.coverage: Secure defaults, transparency, measurable customer security outcomes, and executive ownership of security controls.

standards_alignment.name: NIST AI Risk Management Framework
standards_alignment.coverage: Govern, Map, Measure, and Manage functions for AI lifecycle risk, including governance and operational monitoring.

standards_alignment.name: NIST AI RMF Generative AI Profile
standards_alignment.coverage: Generative AI lifecycle, data, third-party, measurement, monitoring, incident, and operational risk management.

control_checks.control_reason: Clients need machine-readable protected-resource metadata so tokens are minted for the correct MCP resource instead of a generic API.
Authorization server metadata makes issuer, endpoint, supported challenge methods, and token validation assumptions reviewable.
Resource indicators prevent tokens minted for one service from being replayed against another MCP resource.
Audience validation rejects confused-deputy and token-substitution attempts before the MCP server handles a tool call.
PKCE binds authorization-code flows to the client that initiated the grant.
Agents must not receive or forward raw user tokens to downstream tools or hidden connector calls.
Scopes should bind to workflow, namespace, access mode, and run intent rather than broad user or service-account authority.
Session binding makes a tool call traceable to one workflow run and simplifies revocation after kill signals.
Correlation IDs connect authorization, gateway, MCP server, source host, ticket, CI, and run receipt evidence.
Local MCP servers need explicit launch-command control because package or command drift changes the trust boundary.

enterprise_adoption_packet.board_level_claim: SecurityRecipes can verify the authorization boundary around MCP tool use before an agent acts.
default_questions_answered: Which MCP connectors have enough gateway evidence to be used today?
Which candidate MCP servers fail resource, audience, PKCE, token-passthrough, or private-network checks?
Which workflow namespaces are exposed to authorization scope drift?
What runtime attributes must appear in a run receipt before an MCP tool call is trusted?
Which premium controls should be hosted: live metadata discovery, scope-drift alerts, token-binding replay tests, and auth evidence exports?
enterprise_adoption_packet.recommended_first_use: Run this pack during MCP server intake, remote MCP rollout, IAM review, gateway policy design, and quarterly connector recertification.
enterprise_adoption_packet.sales_motion: Lead with open conformance rules, then sell hosted MCP authorization discovery, live metadata checks, fleet-wide scope-drift detection, and signed run-level authorization receipts.
